The behavioral data analysis Framework is organised around three layers.
What each analysis is trying to learn

children.label: What each analysis is trying to learn

children.value: This base laver contains seven generalised linear mixed models (GLMMs), each targeting a different behavioural dependent variable, all estimated on the trial-level dataset. The models share a common predictor structure (centred ordered-condition contrasts, normalised within-episode position

children.value: evidence in this episode), captured by M5. This factor supposed to provide a clean behavioural signature of structural inference vs cue-specific learning.

The remaining models (M1 accuracy, M2 post-transition recovery, M6 placement time, M7 defense rate) characterise the broader behavioural envelope and provide context for interpreting the focal effects.
Early-phase companion analyses (M1_early, M3_early, M4_early, M7_early)

children.label: Early-phase companion analyses (M1_early, M3_early, M4_early, M7_early)

children.value: Alongside the seven primary models, the framework includes early-phase companion analyses on a restricted sample (the first ~25 trials of each participant’s session, in two cuts: “pre-first-transition” and “with-first-transition”). The theoretical motivation is that the manipulation should affect performance most strongly

in the session when condition 3 participants enter with the generative model already constructed and condition 1/2 participants must construct it from data. By the end of the session, conditions may have converged via data-driven learning in conditions 1/2. The full-session models average across this early-divergence/late-convergence regime, which dilutes the manipulation signature. The early-phase models test directly whether the early-trial divergence pattern holds.
The companion models share a learning-curve predictor structure:

Layer B: model-free temporal-dynamics analyses (M_CP, M_SURV)

children.label: Layer B: model-free temporal-dynamics analyses (M_CP, M_SURV)

Layer C: inter-individual variation (BLUP factor structure)

children.label: Layer C: inter-individual variation (BLUP factor structure)

children.value: Per-participant best linear unbiased predictors (BLUPs) from M1–M7 are extracted, assembled into a participant × behavioural-feature matrix, and factor-analysed. The aim is to characterise the latent dimensions of inter-individual variation in behaviour and to test whether they map onto the cognitive parameters that the BayesFlow generative-model fits estimate from the same data (see
). This approach provides a behavioural-side validation of the model-side parameter recovery.
A small number of methodological commitments are applied uniformly across the framework. They are documented once in
and referenced from the model chapters:
